what_went_right: Design/architecture was scoped before any code: the per-viewer (not per-poster) constraint, the local-only/never-shared-back invariant, and the optional_host_permissions-on-user-gesture permission model were all reasoned out and stated up front, honoring the project's decode-protocol/design-before-implement discipline (transcript design block + lib/unfurl.js header comments).

Threat model was understood and documented: attacker-controlled og:* strings, textContent-only rendering, cookieless fetch (credentials:'omit'), no-referrer images, byte/field-length caps, negative caching of dead links. The textContent XSS invariant held as written.

Pure logic was extracted into a node-testable lib/unfurl.js (string-in/data-out), enabling a strong unit suite without a DOM — 500 tests green (up from 469), including adversarial cases (script-bearing og:title verbatim, entity-encoded payload, javascript: image rejected, byte-cap cutoff).

The trending tune was designed as a safe, opt-in, one-line-revert change: DEFAULT_RECENCY_ALPHA=1.0 reproduces the old pure-recency ranking exactly, so callers that don't pass recencyAlpha are unchanged; the new behavior is gated behind the app.js call-site value, with a test asserting default==alpha-1 equivalence.

The per-author frequency cap was added with explicit anti-spam tests (one author 6x capped to 3; 5 distinct authors accumulate to 5; author-less occurrences pooled into one capped bucket) — the spam vector was considered and pinned, not assumed.

Mandatory code review ran before push, returned 0 critical / 3 should-fix / 1 nit, and ALL 3 should-fixes were folded in one pass WITH adversarial tests; the verdict + caught findings were written into the commit body per the persist-review-outcomes rule.

Permission lifecycle handled correctly: chrome.permissions.request kept synchronous inside the Save-click handler to preserve the user gesture, re-checked at boot via chrome.permissions.contains (revocation in chrome:// honored), and relinquished via chrome.permissions.remove on toggle-off.
what_went_wrong: Three security should-fixes on the new attacker-controlled-input surface were caught by the reviewer, not the author, despite the author having written the correct intent in comments: renderUnfurlCard's comment said the image is 'https-only (enforced in parseOgMetadata)' but resolveImageUrl had no scheme check until the fix; attr() matched a data-content shadow attribute (card-text spoof) until a negative-lookbehind was added; readCapped's streaming cap replaced an unbounded res.text() buffer. The comment claimed invariants the code did not yet enforce.

Two unrelated changes (the v0.7 link-preview feature and the trending recency/frequency tune) were committed as separate commits but shipped/reviewed in the same session/diff. Minor — they were cleanly separated into two commits, so this is a note, not a defect.
three_whys: Why 1 (why did it ship clean despite a new security surface?): because the mandatory code-reviewer gate ran before push on a fresh adversarial pass and caught all three claimed-but-unenforced invariants, and every catch was folded with a test that fails without the enforcing line — so the gate, not the author's self-review, closed the gap.

Why 2 (why did the gate have something real to catch rather than rubber-stamping?): because the work was on a genuinely new, security-sensitive surface (cross-origin fetch of attacker-controlled pages) where the author's correct prose intent ('https-only', 'textContent-only') created an anchoring risk — a reader trusts the comment instead of re-verifying the line — so the independent reviewer reasoning from the code, not the comment, found the drift.

Why 3 (why was the author's prose-correct-but-code-incomplete state even possible?): because a security invariant stated in a comment is a SPEC, not a GATE — nothing executable failed when the enforcing line was absent, so the half-built enforcement passed the author's own happy-path tests and only an adversarial test (http-image-rejected, data-content-spoof) would have caught it at author time. That missing same-commit adversarial test is the durable lesson.

drift_checklist.finding: No findings dismissed. All 3 should-fixes were accepted and fixed with tests in one pass; the 1 nit was intentionally left because the reviewer advised it self-heals on the next poll, which is a reviewer-endorsed deferral, not a dismissal.

citations.quote: Three should-fixes, all valid. Fixing all three (the nit self-heals on next poll — reviewer said leave it).

evidence_citations.quote: The image is https-only (enforced in parseOgMetadata) and loaded with no referrer + lazily.
it("is not spoofed by a data-content shadow attribute before content", ...
3 should-fix findings including an invariant violation (https-only for og:image) and an attr() parsing bug that enables card content spoofing; feature is new security-sensitive surface
Fix 2 — resolveImageUrl https-only (matches the comment + invariant; http images would be mixed-content-blocked anyway)

action_items.title: For attacker-controlled-input surfaces, pair each invariant comment with an enforcing line + same-commit adversarial test
action_items.description: When v0.7+ adds any code that fetches/parses attacker-controlled external content (the unfurl path is the first such surface in BetterSSC), require for every security invariant stated in a comment: (a) the enforcing line in the same commit, (b) >=1 adversarial test that fails if that line is removed. The unfurl.test.mjs http-image-rejected + data-content-spoof tests are the model. Apply to any future expansion of the unfurl feature (e.g. allowlisting hosts, image proxying) so the reviewer isn't the first line of security enforcement.
